So I know maybe Nate already kind of given the background of the app, but I'll still give you just a tiny framing in terms of where we are.
So where we are, we fulfill this kind of sweet spot, the Goldilocks intersection spot between building traditional workflows, which is simplified low-code or no-code workflows that can be done.
We've been a we've Northwestern And I'm share my bit, bit go?
the off button.
So we build workflows with agents in the middle, and that's what you kind of see here right now on my screen.
So the classic way, or at least a lot of people today have a choice.
Do they go with something like Google or they go with Copilot or they go with Anthropic or ChatGPT alone?
And in those cases, they wind up getting an agent, and they write paragraphs of text, and it kind of works.

It's great for maybe if you have some questions and answers, but if you want to build any business-centric workflow, especially in regulated industries, they don't do the same thing every time, and it's very difficult to audit what they do.
So unlike traditional code where you can step through it...
That block of text just gets absorbed into those billions of parameters of the GPT or whatever the LLM behind the scenes is, and it just doesn't work for American enterprise.
Then on the flip side, we have these traditional players like ServiceNow or UiPath, which build regular workflows, and they've been around for years.

And recently, they started adopting agents into their workflow, but they're kind of bolt-on, and we really fit into the spot natively because we coincidentally started building out our platform when agents came online.
So we have this, what you see on the screen, an example of a traditional workflow with an agent in the middle.
So this is a workflow for an employee coming in and asking for, hey, can I take a beer?
It's your request, which gets routed to an agent.
Once agent processes it, then we go ahead and put it back into a workflow that goes ahead, make sure that the response matches our expectations and then writes it out to the system like Workday.
And what this is, this is a gateway for, puts us into a very good position to be a gateway for corporate actions.
So there's a lot of, there's a sprawl of agents, even though they're not performing well.
There's still a lot of agents.
if you remember back in the day, there were a lot of websites.

So instead of having this wild, wild jungle of agents all over the place, one in Workday, one in SAP, one in you name it.
So everyone has an agent these days.

We allow companies to create not only just these deterministic workflows, we're well positioned to create this front door or the golden entry for organizations to have us as a front door where we route to their agent, get the response back, and then create this unified front door.

Technical details.
So we, one of our competitive advantages is that we, we're like the Switzerland of, of the platform.
So specifically, we don't have our own agents.
So a customer has to bring your own agent.
So specifically, so for example, I'm going to, I'm an administrator here on the platform.
So I can go into this AI services.
And once it loads, you're going to see a list of variety of agents that a customer will connect that they already working with, whether it's tropic or whatever is.
And the second piece is the database.
So a lot of our customers, interestingly enough, are from regulated industries like pharma or insurance.
And the reason they pick us is because we don't copy the data out of their system.
We work in their system.
So when we install our application, this cloud link thing will usually get like the most popular system like Snowflake.
Or Databricks or Postgres.
So these are kind of the most frequently used systems with us.
But ultimately, the customer owns the database or the data cloud, and we install and live in their cloud.
So and that seems like, wait, why are you doing this?
But it's the comfort.
So the customer has their own database, and we live in there, and we provide that security and also a lot of performance from that.
And the same thing with the agents, right?
So they will have, so most corporations already have contracts with ChatGPT and Anthropic, and they have negotiated a lot of very specific privacy clauses, right?
So we will, so when we go into this AI services piece and I connect to any of these platforms, we are going to, the customer will provide the API key that they own.
And...
Interactions with the Anthropic agents will be according to the terms of that customer.
So that's a huge win for us.
Also, our competitors, they, like, for example, if you go with Google, they will not offer, you can't build an agent that leverages something from OpenAI.
Or if you go to OpenAI or Microsoft, they will only offer OpenAI, but they will not offer the Google.
Same thing with Bedrock from Amazon.
They will only offer a certain subset of agents.
But again, we are like the Switzerland of agents and databases.
And we offer whatever you own.
We just bring it into this one unified UI with governance.
So here you can even see how much money is being spent with each provider.
And again, this is very attractive to our customers.
Yeah, and Aida, I mentioned yesterday that we're working this week on a slightly new rev of the messaging.

And a couple of the really important points are first, fundamentally, when you're building a workflow, there are specific pieces of the workflow that agents are really good at, and we allow you to build deterministic logic for things like routing or branching logic or anything that's really rules-based, where you could maybe get an agent to do the same thing, but it's dramatically more expensive.
And it's probably not as reliable because of the probabilistic nature of LLMs.
So it's first, only use agents generally, where you need to use an agent in the workflow.

And then we allow you to pick which of the LLMs, which of the service providers, which of, you know, is it a Snowflake agent, is it a ServiceNow agent, is it ChatGPT, and use the agent that's most appropriate for that step.
And then...
And one of the pieces of messaging that's resonating really well with CIOs is, like, if you need the newest, most sophisticated, most expensive model, great, use it.
If it's a task that doesn't require that, use DeepSeq, right?
Use an older model that's a lot cheaper and lighter weight.

And that idea of only apply the big, sophisticated agent hammer where you really need it and otherwise use agent processing that's a lot more efficient and cheaper, that's landing really, really well.
Obviously, we're working on the words to say that in a lot more succinct way.
Yeah.

mean, this is great.

And then the second piece is that we live in the customer's data cloud.
That's a very big comfort point and performance points.
